practice_questions.question_text: A 70-year-old male presents with an acutely painful, swollen right knee that developed over 2–3 days without any known cause. There is no other joint pain. He has had similar, less severe episodes off and on in the past. He feels well otherwise and has not had any fever, chills, or rash. Examination of other joints is negative except for some degenerative changes of the distal interphalangeal joints of the fingers. Examination of the right knee is notable for warmth, redness, diffuse tenderness, and swelling. There is no evidence of knee instability, meniscal injury, or trauma. A knee x-ray shows soft-tissue swelling and chondrocalcinosis (calcification of the cartilage). Knee aspiration is performed and a synovial fluid Gram stain is negative for bacteria with other results pending. Which one of the following is the most likely explanation for these knee findings? (check one)
practice_questions.explanation: These symptoms and radiographic findings are most consistent with calcium pyrophosphate deposition (CPPD) disease, also known as pseudogout. CPPD disease is an asymmetric, inflammatory, pauciarticular arthritis most frequently involving the knee, wrist, or first metatarsophalangeal joint. Inflammatory symptoms include erythema, warmth, swelling, and tenderness. The differential diagnosis includes CPPD disease, gout, and septic arthritis. The chondrocalcinosis, or calcification of the cartilage, is characteristic of CPPD disease and not gout. On polarized microscopy of joint fluid, CPPD disease is confirmed by positively birefringent crystals whereas gout is confirmed by negatively birefringent crystals. Osteoarthritis is noninflammatory in nature. There is no history of psoriasis in this patient and the history and physical examination are not suggestive of psoriatic arthritis. Septic arthritis is less likely as this patient’s Gram stain is negative for bacteria and the patient has not had a fever.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["Calcium pyrophosphate deposition (CPPD) disease", "Pseudogout", "Gout", "Septic arthritis", "Osteoarthritis"], "drugs": [], "guidelines": ["AAFP Board Review", "Systematic approach to diagnosis of arthritis"], "thresholds": ["Acute presentation over 2-3 days", "Asymmetric, pauciarticular involvement", "Positively birefringent crystals on polarized microscopy"], "concept_summary": "Distinguishing CPPD disease (pseudogout) from gout and septic arthritis based on clinical presentation, radiographic findings of chondrocalcinosis, and crystal analysis on polarized microscopy."}

practice_questions.question_text: A healthy 49-year-old female presents to your office for a routine health maintenance visit. Since her last visit a year ago she has had only two menstrual periods. She reports sudden sensations of extreme heat in her face, neck, and chest that last just a few minutes but occur throughout the day. These symptoms are very bothersome and interfere with the quality of her sleep. Which one of the following would you recommend to relieve her symptoms? (check one)
practice_questions.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "Black cohosh"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Combined estrogen and progesterone"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Compounded bioidentical hormones"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Micronized progesterone"}]
practice_questions.explanation: This patient presents with typical vasomotor symptoms that can begin in perimenopause and affect sleep quality. Hormone therapy is the gold standard for treatment of vasomotor symptoms. Combination estrogen and progesterone therapy is highly effective for vasomotor symptoms and provides protection against uterine neoplasia. Although micronized progesterone decreases vasomotor symptoms there are no long-term studies to assess the safety of progestin-only treatment for menopausal symptoms. Compounded bioidentical hormone therapy creates safety concerns and is not a first-line therapy due to limited government regulation and monitoring, the potential for overdosing and underdosing, impurities or lack of sterility, and the lack of labeling describing risks. Testosterone alone is not FDA-approved for use in women. Additionally, it has not been shown to be beneficial for treatment of vasomotor symptoms in combination with hormone therapy and is associated with significant side effects. It may be useful for hypoactive sexual desire in postmenopausal women. There is insufficient data to recommend the use of herbal remedies such as black cohosh.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["Perimenopause", "Vasomotor symptoms (hot flashes)", "Menopause-related sleep disturbance"], "drugs": ["Hormone therapy (estrogen-progesterone combination)", "Combination estrogen and progesterone therapy", "Micronized progesterone", "Progestin-only therapy", "Bioidentical hormone therapy", "Testosterone"], "guidelines": ["North American Menopause Society (NAMS) 2017 hormone therapy position statement", "Endocrine Society clinical practice guideline - Androgen therapy in women", "FDA approval status for hormone therapies"], "thresholds": ["Two menstrual periods in one year (oligomenorrhea indicating perimenopause)"], "concept_summary": "Recognition of vasomotor symptoms in perimenopause and selection of hormone therapy as first-line treatment, with understanding of appropriate formulations and safety profiles of different hormone replacement options."}

practice_questions.question_text: A 33-year-old male presents for follow-up of long-standing heartburn. His symptoms have been present for 3 years and recent treatment for 1 month with pantoprazole (Protonix) has only provided partial relief. He has a history of allergic rhinitis that is treated with fluticasone propionate nasal spray (Flonase). He has had dysphagia with solids, and a food impaction 3 months ago that resolved spontaneously. Upper endoscopy reveals eosinophilic esophagitis. Which one of the following is most likely to improve this patient’s symptoms?
practice_questions.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "A clotrimazole oropharyngeal lozenge"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Budesonide oral suspension (Eohilia)"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Oral famotidine (Pepcid)"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Oral metoclopramide (Reglan)"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "Oral montelukast (Singulair)"}]
practice_questions.explanation: Eosinophilic esophagitis is an inflammatory condition triggered by food antigens in sensitive individuals. Endoscopic findings of linear grooves, concentric rings, and whitish exudates may suggest its presence, but the diagnosis is confirmed histologically based on the presence of >15 eosinophils/hpf and the exclusion of other causes of eosinophilia. The diagnosis should be suspected in patients with heartburn who have atopic illnesses such as allergic rhinitis, asthma, or eczema and who do not respond to proton pump inhibitor therapy. Eosinophilic esophagitis is more common in men in their 20s and 30s, and dysphagia may be present for 4 or more years before diagnosis. More than half of patients with this condition may have a history of food impaction. First-line pharmacologic treatment is oral budesonide. Alternatively, inhalant corticosteroids may be sprayed into the oral cavity during a breath hold and then swallowed to minimize pulmonary deposition. Treatment with proton pump inhibitors may help, but they do not provide complete relief in two-thirds of cases. Dietary changes can be very helpful, but consultation with an allergist may be needed. Immunosuppressed individuals such as patients with HIV or diabetes mellitus may have Candida esophagitis, which is treated with fluconazole rather than clotrimazole. Adding an H -blocker such as famotidine would help in cases of erosive esophagitis. Metoclopramide 2 was previously used to treat severe GERD, but it is no longer encouraged due to side effects. While montelukast is used to treat asthma and allergic rhinitis, it is not as effective for eosinophilic esophagitis.
practice_questions.reference: Kroch DA, Madanick RD. Medical treatment of gastroesophageal reflux disease. World J Surg. 2017;41(7):1678-1684. | Clayton S, Emerson JF. Eosinophilic esophagitis: a mimic of gastroesophageal reflux disease. Am Fam Physician. 2018;97(10):628-629. | Mohamed AA, Lu XL, Mounmin FA. Diagnosis and treatment of esophageal candidiasis: current updates. Can J Gastroenterol Hepatol. 2019;2019:3585136. | Gmez-Aldana A, Jaramillo-Santos M, Delgado A, Jaramillo C, Luez-Mindiola A. Eosinophilic esophagitis: current concepts in diagnosis and treatment. World J Gastroenterol. 2019;25(32):4598-4613. | Arnold MJ. Eosinophilic esophagitis: management guidelines from the AGA and JTF. Am Fam Physician. 2021;103(9):573-574.

practice_questions.question_text: A 17-year-old female with well-controlled diabetes mellitus is brought to your office by her mother for a sports preparticipation physical evaluation prior to playing soccer. She has intermittent knee pain when running but no other symptoms. Which one of the following, if present in this patient, would be a CONTRAINDICATION to playing soccer?
practice_questions.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "A BMI >30 kg/m2"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "An enlarged spleen"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Insulin-dependent diabetes"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Mitral valve prolapse"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "Tibial tubercle apophysitis"}]
practice_questions.explanation: A sports preparticipation evaluation is important for assessing health risks and ensuring an athlete is not unnecessarily restricted from play. An athlete with an acutely enlarged spleen or liver faces an increased risk of organ rupture during contact sports. This patient should be evaluated for a cause of the organomegaly, if present, prior to participation. Obesity in an athlete increases the risk for heat-related illness, but it is not an indication to restrict play. Exercise is beneficial for adolescents with diabetes mellitus and may require modification of insulin and eating regimens. Asymptomatic mild mitral valve prolapse is not a reason to deny participation. Tibial tubercle apophysitis is not a contraindication to play, and most experts encourage activity and conservative management.
practice_questions.reference: Rice SG; American Academy of Pediatrics Council on Sports Medicine and Fitness. Medical conditions affecting sports participation. Pediatrics. 2008;121(4):841-848. | Bernhardt DT, Roberts WO, eds; American Academy of Family Physicians. PPE: Preparticipation Physical Evaluation. 5th ed. American Academy of Pediatrics; 2019.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["splenomegaly", "organomegaly"], "drugs": [], "guidelines": [], "thresholds": ["BMI >30 kg/m2"], "concept_summary": "This question tests sports participation contraindications, specifically that enlarged spleen poses significant risk of organ rupture during contact sports and is an absolute contraindication to participation."}

practice_questions.question_text: You are called to the emergency department (ED) to admit a 48-year-old female with a past medical history of hyperthyroidism who presented with agitation, palpitations, and diarrhea. The ED team diagnosed thyroid storm secondary to the abrupt discontinuation of the patient's methimazole 2 weeks ago due to a lapse in insurance. On examination, she is tachycardic and hypertensive but can sustain a conversation without dyspnea. Laboratory studies are significant for elevated free T and T levels and an 3 4 undetectable TSH level. A urine pregnancy test is negative. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate initial step in management?
practice_questions.explanation: Thyroid storm is a medical emergency that must be treated promptly. The initial treatment for thyroid storm is propylthiouracil (PTU). While PTU has been shown to decrease circulating T hormone levels more quickly than methimazole, they have both been shown 3 to be equally effective clinically. Glucocorticoids can also be used to block the conversion of T to T . Hydrocortisone and dexamethasone, not prednisone, are the preferred 4 3 glucocorticoids if used in addition to PTU. While iodine is used to block the synthesis of new thyroid hormone, it should be started at least 1 hour after the initiation of PTU. Plasmapheresis is reserved for patients who do not respond to pharmacologic treatment.
practice_questions.reference: De Leo S, Lee SY, Braverman LE. Hyperthyroidism. Lancet. 2016;388(10047):906-918. | Ross DS, Burch HB, Cooper DS, et al. 2016 American Thyroid Association guidelines for diagnosis and management of hyperthyroidism and other causes of thyrotoxicosis. Thyroid. 2016;26(10):1343-1421. | Lee SY, Modzelewski KL, Law AC, Walkey AJ, Pearce EN, Bosch NA. Comparison of propylthiouracil vs methimazole for thyroid storm in critically ill patients. JAMA Netw Open. 2023;6(4):e238655.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["thyroid storm"], "drugs": ["propylthiouracil", "methimazole", "prednisone", "hydrocortisone"], "guidelines": [], "thresholds": [], "concept_summary": "Tests recognition that propylthiouracil is the initial treatment for thyroid storm, working faster than methimazole to decrease circulating T3 levels."}

practice_questions.question_text:
A 30-year-old soccer player presents with a hyperextension injury of his right wrist after falling and 
landing on his outstretched hand. On examination the wrist is swollen and tender over the anatomic 
snuffbox. A plain radiograph shows soft tissue swelling. 
 
In addition to ice and elevation, which one of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment?
practice_questions.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "Gutter splint"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Thumb spica splint"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Volar splint"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Short arm cast"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "Long arm cast"}]
practice_questions.explanation:
The patient should be presumed to have a scaphoid fracture and treated with a thumb 
spica splint for up to 8 weeks. An initial plain radiograph may be normal in up to 20% 
of scaphoid fractures. Radiography can be repeated in 2 weeks if the wrist remains 
tender. A gutter splint, volar splint, short arm cast, and long arm cast would not be 
appropriate for treatment of a scaphoid fracture.  
 
Reference 
Eiff MP, Hatch R. Fracture Management for Primary Care. 3rd ed. Elsevier Saunders; 
2018:84-90.
practice_questions.reference: Eiff MP, Hatch R: Fracture Management for Primary Care, ed 3 updated. Elsevier Saunders, 2018, pp 201-203.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["scaphoid fracture"], "drugs": [], "guidelines": [], "thresholds": ["20% normal initial radiographs", "8 weeks treatment", "2 weeks repeat imaging"], "concept_summary": "Tests recognition of scaphoid fracture based on anatomic snuffbox tenderness and mechanism, requiring thumb spica splinting even with normal initial radiographs."}

practice_questions.question_text: A 35-year-old female presents because of a 4-week history of difficulty sleeping following the death of her partner of 2 years. She has difficulty initiating and maintaining sleep and reports daytime sleepiness. Her symptoms have not improved despite attending grief counseling sessions. She requests nonpharmacologic treatment for her insomnia. Which one of the following is a component of sleep hygiene education for cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia?
practice_questions.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "Decreasing time spent in bed"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Increasing daytime napping"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Waking up at the same time every day"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Placing a digital clock next to the bed"}]
practice_questions.correct_text: Waking up at the same time every day
practice_questions.explanation: Acute insomnia is a sleep continuity disturbance, defined as difficulty initiating and/or maintaining sleep, that occurs â‰¥3 days per week with a duration of 1 week to 3 months. It is often associated with a precipitating factor such as life events or psychological distress. The first step in the management of acute insomnia is identifying and treating the underlying cause. Cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) is an effective treatment for insomnia and may be offered as first-line therapy, especially in patients who wish to avoid pharmacotherapy. CBT-I has 3 categories: sleep hygiene education, sleep restriction, and stimulus control. Waking up at the same time every day and not napping during the day are among the 8 components of sleep hygiene education. Stimulus control strategies include going to bed only when sleepy and reserving the bedroom for sleep and sex. Sleep restriction techniques involve reducing time spent in bed followed by gradually increasing it by 15 minutes each week once sleep efficiency reaches 90%.
practice_questions.reference: Vargas I, Nguyen AM, Muench A, Bastien CH, Ellis JG, Perlis ML. Acute and chronic insomnia: what has time and/or hyperarousal got to do with it? Brain Sci. 2020;10(2):71.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["acute insomnia", "grief-related insomnia"], "drugs": [], "guidelines": [], "thresholds": ["\u22653 days per week", "1 week to 3 months duration"], "concept_summary": "Tests sleep hygiene components for insomnia management, specifically that maintaining consistent wake time is a key element of sleep hygiene and cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia."}

practice_questions.question_text: A 45-year-old male with a recent diagnosis of Crohn disease presents to your office for a health maintenance examination. His gastroenterologist advised that he ask you about vaccine updates. He has not seen a primary care physician in over 15 years. His current medications include methotrexate and azathioprine (Imuran). He received a Tdap vaccine 6 years ago at an urgent care clinic and recently received a seasonal influenza vaccine and an updated COVID-19 vaccine at his gastroenterologist’s office. He is confident that he received all of his childhood vaccines. Which one of the following vaccines should you recommend today?
practice_questions.explanation: Adults with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) should receive a pneumococcal vaccine. Meningococcal vaccine is currently recommended for adolescents with IBD. The MMR vaccine is contraindicated in patients already on immunosuppressive treatment. Because this patient's Tdap vaccine was administered <10 years ago, he is not due for another dose. Similarly, his next dose of zoster vaccine will be due when he is 50 years of age, and there is no recommendation to administer the next vaccine before the age of 50 in patients with IBD.
practice_questions.reference: Farraye FA, Melmed GY, Lichtenstein GR, Kane SV. ACG clinical guideline: preventive care in inflammatory bowel disease. Am J Gastroenterol. 2017;112(2):241-258. | Kobayashi M, Pilishvili T, Farrar JL, et al. Pneumococcal vaccine for adults aged =19 years: recommendations of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices, United States, 2023. MMWR Recomm Rep. 2023;72(3):1-39.v
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["Crohn disease", "inflammatory bowel disease"], "drugs": ["methotrexate", "azathioprine"], "guidelines": [], "thresholds": ["<10 years since Tdap", "age 50 for zoster vaccine"], "concept_summary": "Tests knowledge that IBD patients on immunosuppressants need pneumococcal vaccination, while live vaccines are contraindicated and age-based vaccines follow standard timing."}

practice_questions.question_text:
A 32-year-old male presents with frequent episodes of palpitations, sweating, dizziness, and 
paresthesias associated with an impending sense of doom. These symptoms have occurred several 
times per week and have disrupted his performance at work. After an appropriate evaluation, you 
diagnose panic disorder. 
 
Which one of the following would be most appropriate for initial treatment?
practice_questions.explanation:
The first-line pharmacologic treatment of panic disorder is SSRI and SNRI 
antidepressant medications (SOR A), including the SSRI escitalopram. Evidence has not 
shown bupropion or buspirone to be effective in the treatment of panic disorder. 
Sedating antihistamines such as hydroxyzine also are not recommended for first-line 
management of panic disorder because of limited evidence of effectiveness and 
decreased tolerability due to their anticholinergic effects. Benzodiazepines such as 
lorazepam are less effective than antidepressants but improve symptoms compared to 
placebo, but they carry an increased risk for adverse effects including tolerance, 
dependence, withdrawal, and mortality.  
 
Reference 
DeGeorge KC, Grover M, Streeter GS. Generalized anxiety disorder and panic disorder in 
adults. Am Fam Physician. 2022;106(2):157-164.
practice_questions.reference: DeGeorge KC, Grover M, Streeter GS. Generalized anxiety disorder and panic disorder in adults. Am Fam Physician. 2022;106(2):157-164.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["panic disorder"], "drugs": ["escitalopram", "SSRIs", "SNRIs"], "guidelines": [], "thresholds": [], "concept_summary": "First-line treatment for panic disorder is SSRI or SNRI antidepressants like escitalopram, not benzodiazepines, buspirone, or antihistamines due to better long-term outcomes."}

practice_questions.question_text:
A 77-year-old female with a medical history of atrial fibrillation, COPD, depression, and 
hypothyroidism presents for follow-up of a recent visit to the emergency department (ED) for a fall in 
which she hit her head on the edge of a dresser. CT of the head in the ED was negative for 
intracranial bleeding and she received staples for a scalp laceration. 
Which one of the following medications in this patient's current regimen is most likely to increase her 
fall risk?
practice_questions.explanation:
Use of an SSRI has been found to increase fall risk in older persons. While 
anticoagulants such as apixaban increase the risk for bleeding from fall-related injuries, 
they are recommended to prevent stroke in patients with atrial fibrillation regardless of 
fall risk and have not been noted to increase the risk of falling. Budesonide/formoterol, 
levothyroxine, and prednisone have not been associated with an increased fall risk.  
   
References 
Marcum ZA, Perera S, Thorpe JM, et al. Antidepressant use and recurrent falls in 
community-dwelling older adults: findings from the Health ABC Study. Ann 
Pharmacother. 2016;50(7):525-533.     
 
Moncada LVV, Mire LG. Preventing falls in older persons. Am Fam Physician. 
2017;96(4):240-247.     
 
The 2023 American Geriatrics Society Beers Criteria Update Expert Panel. American 
Geriatrics Society 2023 updated AGS Beers criteria for potentially inappropriate 
medication use in older adults. J Am Geriatr Soc. 2023;71(7):2052-2081.
practice_questions.reference: Marcum ZA, Perera S, Thorpe JM, et al. Antidepressant use and recurrent falls in community-dwelling older adults: findings from the Health ABC Study. Ann Pharmacother. 2016;50(7):525-533. | Moncada LVV, Mire LG. Preventing falls in older persons. Am Fam Physician. 2017;96(4):240-247. | The 2023 American Geriatrics Society Beers Criteria Update Expert Panel. American Geriatrics Society 2023 updated AGS Beers criteria for potentially inappropriate medication use in older adults. J Am Geriatr Soc. 2023;71(7):2052-2081.

practice_questions.question_text: A young couple in your practice presents for preconception counseling. Both partners are known carriers of cystic fibrosis (CF). They ask what risk this poses to their future children. Which one of the following accurately reflects the risk of CF disease transmission to any potential offspring of this couple?
practice_questions.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "25% risk of being affected and 25% risk of being a carrier"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "25% risk of being affected and 50% risk of being a carrier"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "50% risk of being affected and 25% risk of being a carrier"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "50% risk of being affected and 50% risk of being a carrier"}]
practice_questions.correct_text: 25% risk of being affected and 50% risk of being a carrier
practice_questions.explanation: Cystic fibrosis is a genetic disorder with an autosomal recessive pattern of inheritance. People who are affected by the disease are homozygous for the condition, with 2 copies of the mutated gene. Carriers are heterozygous for the condition with 1 mutated gene and 1 normal gene. When both partners in a couple are carriers, any potential offspring has a 25% chance of being affected, a 50% chance of being an unaffected carrier, and a 25% chance of being an unaffected noncarrier.
practice_questions.reference: Dictionary of genetics terms: autosomal recessive inheritance. National Cancer Institute at the National Institutes of Health. Accessed February 16, 2025. | Collins MS, Mansilla-Rivera K. Diagnostic and therapeutic advances in cystic fibrosis: how family physicians can partner in care. Am Fam Physician. 2024;109(5):388-390. | Cystic fibrosis: causes. National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute. Updated November 15, 2024.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["cystic fibrosis"], "drugs": [], "guidelines": [], "thresholds": ["25% affected risk", "50% carrier risk", "25% unaffected risk"], "concept_summary": "Tests understanding of autosomal recessive inheritance patterns and ability to calculate offspring risks when both parents are carriers."}

practice_questions.question_text:
A 30-year-old female presents to your office to discuss fertility and her irregular menses. The patient 
and her husband have been trying to conceive for the past 3 years but have been unsuccessful due to 
her irregular menstrual periods. Physical examination findings are significant for hirsutism and acne 
with hyperpigmentation on the posterior aspect of the neck and in the axillae. Her BMI is 35 kg/m2. 
Laboratory studies from 1 month ago show a hemoglobin A  of 5.0%. 
1c
 
Which one of the following medications would be the most appropriate treatment of this patient’s 
infertility?
practice_questions.explanation:
This patient likely has polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) based on the clinical history 
and physical examination findings. Letrozole is an aromatase inhibitor that decreases 
the amount of estrogen produced by the body and is considered the first-line treatment 
for inducing ovulation in women with PCOS.  
 
GLP-1 receptor agonists reduce the degree of metabolic syndrome in patients with 
PCOS and are not indicated for ovulation induction. Metformin is inferior to letrozole for 
ovulation induction in patients with PCOS. Furthermore, this patient's hemoglobin A  
1c
indicates that she does not have prediabetes or diabetes mellitus. Spironolactone is an 
antiandrogen and is used to treat many PCOS symptoms, such as hirsutism, but does 
not induce ovulation.  
   
References  
Goodman NF, Cobin RH, Futterweit W, et al. American Association of Clinical 
Endocrinologists, American College of Endocrinology, and Androgen Excess and PCOS 
Society Disease State clinical review: guide to the best practices in the evaluation and 
treatment of polycystic ovary syndrome-part 1. Endocr Pract. 2015;21(11):1291-1300.    
 
Goodman NF, Cobin RH, Futterweit W, et al. American Association of Clinical 
Endocrinologists, American College of Endocrinology, and Androgen Excess and PCOS 
Society Disease State clinical review: guide to the best practices in the evaluation and 
treatment of polycystic ovary syndrome-part 2. Endocr Pract. 2015;21(12):1415-1426.    
 
Shrivastava S, Conigliaro RL. Polycystic ovarian syndrome. Med Clin North Am. 
2023;107(2):227-234.     
 
Wilkinson LD, Brady PH, Gin GT, Rosenblum E. Female pelvic conditions: polycystic 
ovary syndrome. FP Essent. 2022;515:26-31.    
 
Williams T, Moore JB, Regehr J. Polycystic ovary syndrome: common questions and 
answers. Am Fam Physician. 2023;107(3):264-272.

Endocr Pract. 2015;21(11):1291-1300. | Goodman NF, Cobin RH, Futterweit W, et al. practice_questions.question_text:
A 55-year-old male presents to your clinic for evaluation of COPD. He has a history of tobacco
use and quit smoking 2 years ago. He reports occasional symptoms that limit his activities but
has  not had any exacerbations or hospitalizations. Pulmonary function tests indicate an
FEV /FVC ratio <0.7 and an FEV  of 75%. His vital signs are normal.
1 1
Which one of the following would be the most appropriate initial pharmacotherapy?
practice_questions.explanation:
COPD is a common condition, which led to 3.23 million deaths worldwide in 2019. A variety of treatments
are available to alleviate symptoms, and family physicians are well suited to manage this condition through
lifestyle modifications and pharmacotherapy. Guidelines suggest that patients with mild disease, as in this
patient’s case per the Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease (GOLD) class 2 findings on
pulmonary function tests and no exacerbations, are best managed through once-daily inhalation of a
long-acting  muscarinic  antagonist  (LAMA)  such  as  tiotropium.  Short-acting  medications  such  as
ipratropium and levalbuterol require frequent dosing and lack the mortality benefit seen with LAMAs. The
combination of LAMAs and long-acting  -agonists (LABAs) can be initiated in those with persistent
symptoms but would not be used as initial therapy. Inhaled corticosteroids may be beneficial in those with
significant asthma and COPD overlap, but in general corticosteroids should be reserved for those with
persistent symptoms despite LAMA and LABA therapy.
 42
Ref: Gentry  S,  Gentry  B.  Chronic  obstructive  pulmonary  disease:  diagnosis  and  management.  Am  Fam  Physician.
2017;95(7):433-441. 2) Celli BR, Wedzicha JA. Update on clinical aspects of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. N
Engl J Med. 2019;381(13):1257-1266. 3) Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease. 2023 Report: global
strategy for the diagnosis, management, and prevention of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Updated 2023.
practice_questions.reference: Gentry S, Gentry B: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease: Diagnosis and management. Am Fam Physician 2017;95(7):433-441. | Celli BR, Wedzicha JA. Updated 2023.

practice_questions.question_text: A 45-year-old female with a past medical history of obesity and cigarette smoking presents with a popliteal deep vein thrombosis of the right leg, confirmed on ultrasonography. She has no prior history of venous thromboembolism and has not had any recent surgery, trauma, or immobilization. She does not take any medications. Which one of the following anticoagulation regimens would be the most appropriate initial treatment?
practice_questions.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "A direct-acting oral anticoagulant (DOAC) only"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH) only"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Warfarin only"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "LMWH for 7 days, followed by warfarin"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "Unfractionated heparin for 48 hours, followed by a DOAC"}]
practice_questions.explanation: Direct-acting oral anticoagulants (DOACs) are now recommended as monotherapy for outpatient management of deep vein thrombosis. DOACs reach therapeutic levels quickly; therefore, concomitant initiation of unfractionated or low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH) is not necessary. DOACs have less major bleeding and require less monitoring, making them preferred over warfarin. Previously, unfractionated heparin was administered during hospitalization while warfarin was simultaneously initiated; however, subcutaneous LMWH can now be started with warfarin as an outpatient and continued until the goal INR is reached if a DOAC is unaffordable.
practice_questions.reference: Stevens SM, Woller SC, Kreuziger LB, et al. Antithrombotic therapy for VTE disease: compendium and review of CHEST guidelines 2012-2021. Chest. 2024;166(2):388-404.

practice_questions.question_text:
A 25-year-old male with no significant past medical history comes to your office to establish
care. A physical examination reveals a cardiac murmur that has not been documented previously.
Which one of the following findings would be most concerning for hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
as the cause of his murmur?
practice_questions.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "A diastolic murmur that increases in the left lateral decubitus position"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "A systolic murmur that increases with an isometric handgrip"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "A systolic murmur that increases when moving from squatting to standing"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "A systolic murmur that decreases with the Valsalva maneuver"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "A fixed split of the second heart sound"}]
practice_questions.correct_text: A systolic murmur that increases when moving from squatting to standing
practice_questions.explanation:
Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) is the leading cause of sudden cardiac death in young people. HCM
often presents with symptoms related to exertion, including dyspnea, chest pain, palpitations, or syncope,
although it also may be discovered incidentally based on its characteristic findings on physical examination
and/or EKG abnormalities. Because only 30%”“60% of people with HCM are found to have a genetic
variant, family physicians play a key role in the early detection of new cases. The cardiac structural
changes with HCM usually lead to a dynamic left ventricular outflow tract obstruction (LVOTO) that
worsens with decreased preload and decreased afterload states. An LVOTO causes a harsh midsystolic
ejection murmur that intensifies with exertion or with provocative maneuvers that increase contractility or
decrease cardiac preload and/or afterload. One such method is to have the patient move from a squatting
position to a standing position. This leads to temporary pooling of blood in the legs, decreasing venous
return and preload and thereby increasing the relative obstruction and the intensity of the murmur. The
Valsalva maneuver also causes reduced preload by decreasing left ventricular filling, thus increasing the
relative obstruction and the murmur intensity. An isometric handgrip, in contrast, increases afterload and
decreases the obstruction, resulting in a softer murmur. A diastolic murmur that increases in the left lateral
decubitus position is associated with aortic regurgitation. A fixed split of the second heart sound occurs
due to an atrial septal defect.
Ref: Authors/Task Force members, Elliott PM, Anastasakis A, et al. 2014 ESC guidelines on diagnosis and management of
hypertrophic cardiomyopathy: the task force for the diagnosis and management of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy of the
European Society of Cardiology (ESC). Eur Heart J. 2014;35(39):2733-2779. 2) Marian AJ, Braunwald E. Hypertrophic
cardiomyopathy: genetics, pathogenesis, clinical manifestations, diagnosis, and therapy. Circ Res. 2017;121(7):749-770.
3) Leggit JC, Whitaker D. Diagnosis and management of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy: updated guidelines from the
ACC/AHA. Am Fam Physician. 2022;105(2):207-209.
 48
practice_questions.reference: Authors/Task Force members, Elliott PM, Anastasakis A, et al. 2014 ESC guidelines on diagnosis and management of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy: the task force for the diagnosis and management of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy of the European Society of Cardiology (ESC). Eur Heart J. 2014;35(39):2733-2779. | Marian AJ, Braunwald E. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy: genetics, pathogenesis, clinical manifestations, diagnosis, and therapy. Circ Res. 2017;121(7):749-770. | Leggit JC, Whitaker D. Diagnosis and management of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy: updated guidelines from the ACC/AHA. Am Fam Physician. 2022;105(2):207-209.

practice_questions.question_text:
A 63-year-old female with a history of obesity and hypertension sees you for evaluation of
shortness of breath on exertion and lower extremity edema. Echocardiography shows grade 2
diastolic dysfunction and an ejection fraction of 50%. You diagnose heart failure with preserved
ejection fraction.
Which one of the following medications has the best evidence to reduce hospitalization due to
heart failure or cardiovascular death in patients such as this?
practice_questions.explanation:
Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF), defined as an EF ï‚³50%, has a relative paucity of
evidence-based treatments leading to improved patient outcomes compared to heart failure with reduced
ejection fraction (HFrEF), defined as an EF <40%. While all of the options listed have good evidence
of benefit in HFrEF, only the SGLT2 inhibitor empagliflozin has been shown to improve the composite
outcome of hospitalization due to heart failure or cardiovascular death in HFpEF. The 2022 American
Heart Association/American College of Cardiology/Heart Failure Society of America guideline for the
management of heart failure recommends SGLT2 inhibitors as having the best evidence of benefit in
HFpEF.
-Blockers such as carvedilol may be used for rate control in patients with atrial fibrillation and HFpEF
but are not clearly beneficial otherwise. Clinical trials of ACE inhibitors such as lisinopril and angiotensin
receptor blockers such as valsartan have not shown improved outcomes for patients with HFpEF.
Sacubitril/valsartan similarly did not achieve the primary end point of improvement in time to HF
hospitalization or cardiovascular death in this patient population. The mineralocorticoid antagonist
spironolactone is associated with improved diastolic function in patients with HFpEF and was found to
improve hospitalizations but not cardiovascular death as a primary outcome.
Ref: Pitt B, Pfeffer MA, Assmann SF, et al. Spironolactone for heart failure with preserved ejection fraction. N Engl J Med.
2014;370(15):1383-1392. 2) Empagliflozin (Jardiance) for heart failure with reduced ejection fraction. Med Lett Drugs Ther.
2021;63(1636):171-172. 3) Anker SD, Butler J, Filippatos G, et al. Empagliflozin in heart failure with a preserved ejection
fraction. N Engl J Med. 2021;385(16):1451-1461. 4) Heidenreich PA, Bozkurt B, Aguilar D, et al. 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA
guideline for the management of heart failure: a report of the American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association
joint committee on clinical practice guidelines. Circulation. 2022;145(18):e895-e1032.
practice_questions.reference: Pitt B, Pfeffer MA, Assmann SF, et al. Spironolactone for heart failure with preserved ejection fraction. N Engl J Med. 2014;370(15):1383-1392. | Empagliflozin (Jardiance) for heart failure with reduced ejection fraction. Med Lett Drugs Ther. 2021;63(1636):171-172. | Anker SD, Butler J, Filippatos G, et al. Empagliflozin in heart failure with a preserved ejection fraction. N Engl J Med. 2021;385(16):1451-1461. | Heidenreich PA, Bozkurt B, Aguilar D, et al. 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA guideline for the management of heart failure: a report of the American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association joint committee on clinical practice guidelines. Circulation. 2022;145(18):e895-e1032.

practice_questions.question_text:
A 49-year-old female presents to your office with severe left ear pain. The patient describes the pain 
as sharp and deep inside her ear and reports that it has been occurring occasionally for several years. 
The pain has become more constant over the last 2 months, and she currently rates it as 8 out of 10 on 
a pain scale. She has not previously sought an evaluation. 
 
On physical examination you find a normal ear canal and tympanic membrane. The patient is only 
able to open her mouth 2 finger widths, has clicking over the left temporomandibular joint with 
opening, and has pain with palpation of the submandibular musculature. 
 
Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this disorder?
practice_questions.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "Reassurance that her ear is normal, and follow-up in 4 weeks if symptoms have not improved"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "A trial of naproxen and cyclobenzaprine"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Noncontrast CT of the facial bones"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Referral to an otolaryngologist for further evaluation"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "Referral to a maxillofacial surgeon for consideration of jaw realignment surgery"}]
practice_questions.correct_text: A trial of naproxen and cyclobenzaprine
practice_questions.explanation:
There is no need for imaging or further workup to determine the cause of pain in this 
patient with uncomplicated temporomandibular joint dysfunction with muscle spasms. 
The initial treatment is naproxen for inflammation and pain, as well as cyclobenzaprine 
for muscle spasms. Scheduling a follow-up visit without treatment is not appropriate in 
a patient with a clear diagnosis and available medications that can safely provide relief. 
A referral to either an otolaryngologist or a maxillofacial surgeon should be deferred 
until a trial of medication is completed.  
 
Reference   
Matheson EM, Fermo JD, Blackwelder RS. Temporomandibular disorders: rapid evidence 
review. Am Fam Physician. 2023;107(1):52-58.
practice_questions.reference: Matheson EM, Fermo JD, Blackwelder RS. Temporomandibular disorders: rapid evidence review. Am Fam Physician. 2023;107(1):52-58.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["temporomandibular joint dysfunction", "TMJ dysfunction"], "drugs": ["naproxen", "cyclobenzaprine"], "guidelines": [], "thresholds": ["pain 8/10"], "concept_summary": "Tests initial treatment of uncomplicated TMJ dysfunction with muscle spasm using NSAIDs for inflammation and muscle relaxants for spasm without need for imaging or referral."}

practice_questions.question_text:
A 3-month-old male is brought to your office by his parents with a 6-week history of stridor that 
worsens when the infant is lying down and when he eats. On examination the infant appears well and 
is in no respiratory distress. His lungs are clear, and his growth curve is stable at the 85th percentile 
for both weight and length. You note subtle stridor with each inhalation that is more pronounced 
when he feeds. 
 
Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
practice_questions.explanation:
This well-appearing child with chronic stridor most likely has the chronic condition 
laryngomalacia, in which the supraglottic structures collapse over the trachea during 
inhalation and result in the characteristic stridor. Symptoms typically begin between 1 
and 2 months of age and are often chronic. Affected children are otherwise well. The 
stridor may be more pronounced with eating, and in some cases can lead to impaired 
weight gain or choking, cyanosis, or aspiration with feeding. As the child grows, the 
trachea will also grow and the condition will resolve.  
 
Bacterial tracheitis, croup, and epiglottitis are all acute, infectious causes of stridor. 
Foreign body aspiration is typically an acute presentation that is more common in older 
children who are able to grasp and manipulate foreign objects more easily.  
 
Reference 
Skirko J. Childhood respiratory conditions: stridor. FP Essent. 2022;513:25-31.
practice_questions.reference: Skirko J. Childhood respiratory conditions: stridor. FP Essent. 2022;513:25-31.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["laryngomalacia", "stridor", "bacterial tracheitis", "croup", "epiglottitis"], "drugs": [], "guidelines": [], "thresholds": ["symptoms begin 1-2 months of age", "3-month-old"], "concept_summary": "Tests recognition that chronic inspiratory stridor in a well-appearing infant that worsens with feeding and supine positioning indicates laryngomalacia."}

practice_questions.question_text: Which one of the following antibiotics is most commonly associated with Stevens-Johnson syndrome?
practice_questions.explanation: Antibiotics are the most common cause of Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS) and toxic epidermal necrolysis, accounting for more than one-quarter of cases worldwide. Sulfonamide antibiotics, such as sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim, are associated with the majority of antibiotic-associated SJS cases worldwide. Antibiotic stewardship is always important, and consideration for both relatively benign and life-threatening side effects should be considered with every prescription written.
practice_questions.reference: Lee EY, Knox C, Phillips EJ. Worldwide prevalence of antibiotic-associated Stevens-Johnson syndrome and toxic epidermal necrolysis: a systematic review and meta-analysis. JAMA Dermatol. 2023;159(4):384-392.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["Stevens-Johnson syndrome", "toxic epidermal necrolysis"], "drugs": ["sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim", "cephalexin", "ciprofloxacin", "fosfomycin", "nitrofurantoin"], "guidelines": [], "thresholds": [], "concept_summary": "Identifies sulfonamide antibiotics, particularly sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim, as the most common antibiotic cause of Stevens-Johnson syndrome."}

practice_questions.question_text: Of the following cognitive biases, which one best describes the practice of prioritizing data that supports an initial impression, even when the initial impression is incorrect?
practice_questions.explanation: Anchoring bias describes the practice of physicians prioritizing information and data that supports their initial impressions, even when these impressions are incorrect. In other words, it is when physicians are biased toward their first impression. Availability bias describes the likelihood of a diagnosis being erroneously linked to how easy it is to think of examples. Implicit bias is defined as attitudes and beliefs about race, ethnicity, age, ability, gender, or other characteristics. Implicit bias operates on an unconscious level and can only be measured indirectly. Outcomes bias refers to the attribution of all results to prior decisions, regardless of whether there is a reason to do so.
practice_questions.reference: Doherty TS, Carroll AE. Believing in overcoming cognitive biases. AMA J Ethics. 2020;22(9):E773-E778. | Sabin JA. Tackling implicit bias in health care. N Engl J Med. 2022;387(2):105-107. | Catalogue of Bias Collaboration; Banerjee A, Nunan D. Availability bias. Catalogue of Bias. 2019.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": [], "drugs": [], "guidelines": [], "thresholds": [], "concept_summary": "Tests understanding of cognitive biases in medical decision-making, specifically recognizing anchoring bias as prioritizing data supporting initial impressions."}

missed_items_detail.question_text: Your practice management team has reviewed the clinic-wide cancer screening rates and found that the frequency of cervical cancer screening appears to have fallen behind other cancer screenings. You are asked to lead a quality improvement project to increase rates of cervical cancer screening for appropriate patients. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate first step?
missed_items_detail.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "Establishing a system for reviewing charts and contacting patients who are overdue for cervical cancer screening"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Evaluating barriers to cervical cancer screening and reporting"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Placing posters with information about cervical cancer screening inside the clinic"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Sending generalized reminders to patients who may qualify for cervical cancer screening"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "Sending generalized reminders to all clinicians regarding cervical cancer screening guidelines"}]
missed_items_detail.correct_text: Evaluating barriers to cervical cancer screening and reporting
missed_items_detail.explanation: Quality improvement is a key area for clinical practice, but initiating these projects can be challenging. Many different methodologies have been used, including the Plan-Do-Study- Act cycle or Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle, but clear identification of the current issues is critical in any framework. In order to plan appropriate interventions, it must first be determined what barriers are responsible for hindering performance in a certain area. If evaluation shows that there is a systemic issue in capturing data, rather than a clinician issue, no other interventions will be beneficial. When used as part of an organizational plan for staffing and clinical procedures, patient-specific reminders and use of the entire health care team are more likely to improve screening uptake than other methods, but the initial step should be delineating any current issues prior to implementing a change. Educating patients about preventive health care and screenings is essential and should be part of routine care. However, general materials such as clinic posters are less likely to increase screening rates than direct physician recommendations or conversations. Sending generalized reminders for preventive care may miss the patients in most critical need of services. General reminders that rely on clinician evaluation of every individual visit may be less effective than patient-specific reminders that can be addressed at the point of care.
missed_items_detail.reference: Stone EG, Morton SC, Hulscher ME, et al. Interventions that increase use of adult immunization and cancer screening services: a meta-analysis. Ann Intern Med. 2002;136(9):641-651. | Alam Z, Cairns JM, Scott M, Dean JA, Janda M. Interventions to increase cervical screening uptake among immigrant women: a systematic review and meta-analysis. PLoS One. 2023;18(6):e0281976.

missed_items_detail.question_text: You are seeing a 32-year-old male in the office. His most recent hemoglobin A was 6.1%, 1c indicating prediabetes. Which one of the following is most likely to prevent the development of diabetes?
missed_items_detail.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "Intensive lifestyle and behavior change program"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Low-carbohydrate diet"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Reduction in daily caloric intake by 250 kcal per day"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Strength training for 90 minutes per week"}]
missed_items_detail.correct_text: Intensive lifestyle and behavior change program
missed_items_detail.explanation: Given the growing prevalence of diabetes mellitus, clinicians should be familiar with effective prevention strategies. Intensive lifestyle and behavior modification programs, such as the National Diabetes Prevention Program, are low-cost and can reduce the development of diabetes, with one U.S. study demonstrating a 27% reduction over 15 years. These programs help patients set weight loss goals â‰¥7% of their baseline, which is thought to be a key component of diabetes prevention. This is typically achieved through a caloric reduction of 500”“1000 kcal per day, with activity goals â‰¥150 minutes per week, including up to 75 minutes per week of strength training. While a variety of dietary patterns are thought to be effective for diabetes prevention, including low intake of carbohydrates, the evidence for changes in dietary patterns is not as strong as that of intensive lifestyle programs.
missed_items_detail.reference: American Diabetes Association Professional Practice Committee. 3. Prevention or delay of diabetes and associated comorbidities: standards of care in diabetes�2025. Diabetes Care. 2025;48(Supplement_1):S50-S58.

missed_items_detail.question_text: A 78-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of cough and fever and a 2-hour history of confusion. Her vital signs include a weight of 67 kg (148 lb), a temperature of 38.8 °C (101.8 °F), a pulse rate of 122 beats/min, and a blood pressure of 72/44 mm Hg with a mean arterial pressure (MAP) of 53 mm Hg. A chest x-ray shows left lower lobe pneumonia. She is given 2 L of lactated Ringer solution and started on broad- spectrum antibiotics for sepsis secondary to pneumonia. Her blood pressure 3 hours later is 82/46 mm Hg with a MAP of 58 mm Hg. Which one of the following is the most appropriate immediate next step in management?
missed_items_detail.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "A 1-L bolus of normal saline"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "A dopamine infusion"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "A norepinephrine infusion"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "A vasopressin infusion"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "Placement of a central line"}]
missed_items_detail.explanation: This patient is in septic shock secondary to pneumonia. Surviving Sepsis Campaign guidelines recommend early goal-directed therapy (EGDT) consisting of initial fluid resuscitation with 30 mL/kg of crystalloid solution within the first 3 hours and appropriate antibiotics within 1 hour of evaluation, which have already been done for this patient. The guidelines also recommend initiating vasopressor therapy with norepinephrine as the first- line agent if the mean arterial pressure remains <65 mm Hg after fluid resuscitation. Norepinephrine is a more potent vasoconstrictor than dopamine. Additional fluid therapy is controversial and patient-specific, with some experts recommending discrete 500-mL boluses with hemodynamic monitoring of response, and others recommending balanced crystalloids such as lactate Ringer solution over normal saline. Vasopressin should be added for adults with septic shock who are receiving norepinephrine but continue to have inadequate mean arterial pressure. Vasopressor therapy should not be delayed for placement of a central line.
missed_items_detail.reference: Evans L, Rhodes A, Alhazzani W, et al. Surviving Sepsis Campaign: international guidelines for management of sepsis and septic shock 2021. Critical Care Medicine. 2021;49(11):e1063-e1143.

missed_items_detail.question_text: According to guidelines from the American Gastroenterological Association, which one of the following is recommended as the best pharmacologic treatment of typical symptoms in diarrhea-predominant irritable bowel syndrome?
missed_items_detail.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "Amitriptyline"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Dicyclomine"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Linaclotide (Linzess)"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Lubiprostone (Amitiza)"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "Rifaximin (Xifaxan)"}]
missed_items_detail.explanation: According to the 2022 American Gastroenterological Association guidelines for diarrhea- predominant irritable bowel syndrome (IBS-D), the nonabsorbable antibiotic rifaximin has the best evidence for controlling diarrhea, discomfort, bloating, and abdominal pain. It has a broad spectrum with activity against gram-positive, gram-negative (including Clostridioides difficile), and anaerobic bacteria. It is thought to work by reducing bacterial byproducts and altering the gut flora. It is prescribed as a 14-day course for acute and recurrent symptoms. Rifaximin does not have any significant adverse effects, but it is a high-cost medication. Low-dose amitriptyline and dicyclomine are often effective in reducing symptoms, but the evidence is not as strong as that of rifaximin. Linaclotide is used for constipation- predominant IBS (IBS-C), but its chief side effect of diarrhea results in many patients discontinuing the medication. Lubiprostone is another medication used for IBS-C and for opioid-induced constipation, not IBS-D.
missed_items_detail.reference: Karuppiah S, Pomianowski K. Rifaximin (Xifaxan) for irritable bowel syndrome. Am Fam Physician. 2017;95(4):258-259. | Chang L, Sultan S, Lembo A, Verne GN, Smalley W, Heidelbaugh JJ. AGA clinical practice guideline on the pharmacologic management of irritable bowel syndrome with constipation. Gastroenterology. 2022;163(1):118-136. | Arnold MJ. Medications for irritable bowel syndrome: guidelines from the AGA. Am Fam Physician. 2023;108(5):527-529. | Lembo A, Sultan S, Chang L, Heidelbaugh JJ, Smalley W, Verne GN. AGA clinical practice guideline on the pharmacological management of irritable bowel syndrome with diarrhea. Gastroenterology. 2022;163(1)137-151.

missed_items_detail.question_text: A generally healthy 60-year-old female presents to your office because she is concerned about urinary leakage. Her symptoms started a couple of years ago and have worsened. The leakage occurs when she coughs or laughs, but can also happen if she waits too long to urinate. She hydrates throughout the day. She does not take any medications and does not smoke. She has a remote surgical history of a hysterectomy after delivering her third child. She does not have any fever or abdominal pain and has not been exposed to any sexually transmitted infections. Recent laboratory studies, including a CBC, comprehensive metabolic panel, TSH level, and hemoglobin A , were unremarkable. Today she has a blood pressure 1c of 120/70 mm Hg and a BMI of 32 kg/m2. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step in the workup?
missed_items_detail.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "No testing"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Urinalysis"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Renal and bladder ultrasonography"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Urodynamic testing"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "Cystoscopy"}]
missed_items_detail.explanation: Urinary incontinence is common among women and can substantially diminish quality of life while contributing to increased financial strain. Risk factors include obesity, parity, increasing age, and a history of hysterectomy. The initial evaluation includes a thorough history and should establish whether the incontinence is transient or chronic. It should also identify the incontinence subtype as well as any red flags that would warrant subspecialist referral, such as pelvic organ prolapse or fistula. Urinalysis should be ordered for all patients with urinary incontinence. An incontinence screening questionnaire, cough stress test (SOR C), postvoid residual measurement, and a 3-day voiding diary are helpful tools. Renal and bladder ultrasonography, urodynamic testing, and cystoscopy are not recommended for the initial evaluation of uncomplicated urinary incontinence. Treatments include weight loss, pelvic floor training, lifestyle modifications including appropriate fluid intake, smoking cessation, and pharmacologic therapy. Patients with refractory urinary incontinence can be referred for more invasive treatments, such as mechanical devices, intravesical onabotulinumtoxinA injection, and surgical procedures.
missed_items_detail.reference: Hu JS, Pierre EF: Urinary incontinence in women: Evaluation and management. Am Fam Physician 2019;100(6):339-348.

missed_items_detail.question_text: A 68-year-old female with previously diagnosed immune thrombocytopenia presents to your office to establish care. She has no current bleeding. A CBC is normal except for a platelet count of 47,000/mm3 (N 130,000”“400,000). You refer her to a hematologist for long-term management. Which one of the following is the most appropriate treatment for this patient's current thrombocytopenia?
missed_items_detail.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "No treatment"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Oral hydroxychloroquine sulfate (Plaquenil)"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Oral prednisone"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Intravenous methylprednisolone sodium succinate (Solu-Medrol)"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "Intravenous rituximab (Rituxan)"}]
missed_items_detail.explanation: Immune thrombocytopenia (ITP) is caused by autoimmune destruction of platelets in the spleen and causes chronic thrombocytopenia of varying severity. Although hematologists typically oversee care for patients with ITP, family physicians may need to make acute treatment decisions in collaboration with these patients. The American Society of Hematology recommends not to treat ITP unless there is active bleeding or very low platelet counts, defined as <30,000/mm3. This patient does not require treatment at this time because she does not have any current bleeding and her platelet count is above the threshold for treatment. Corticosteroids and rituximab are both used to treat ITP, along with intravenous immunoglobulin. Hydroxychloroquine sulfate is not typically recommended in the treatment of ITP.
missed_items_detail.reference: O'Gurek D, Choi HYJ: Hemoptysis: Evaluation and management. Am Fam Physician 2022;105(2):144-151.

missed_items_detail.question_text: A 40-year-old male with a history of alcohol use disorder presents to your office for help abstaining from alcohol. Until recently, he has been drinking at least 6 beers per day and often more on weekend days. He has 2 prior arrests for DUI, and his wife plans to file for divorce if he does not change. Over the past 2 weeks, he has been able to wean himself off alcohol and has joined a sobriety group. He requests medication to help him maintain his sobriety. Which one of the following medications should you recommend for this patient?
missed_items_detail.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "Acamprosate"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Disulfiram"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Gabapentin (Neurontin)"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Topiramate (Topamax)"}]
missed_items_detail.explanation: This patient meets criteria for moderate alcohol use disorder (AUD). Although AUD is a leading cause of preventable death, less than 2% of patients with AUD are prescribed medications. Acamprosate is recommended for patients who have achieved abstinence and desire to maintain it (SOR A). Gabapentin is useful for the prevention of withdrawal and is safe for those who are still drinking (SOR A). Topiramate is useful in decreasing heavy drinking (SOR B). There is insufficient evidence to recommend disulfiram.
missed_items_detail.reference: Poorman E, McQuade BM, Messmer S. Medications for alcohol use disorder. Am Fam Physician. 2024;109(1):71-78.

missed_items_detail.question_text: A 15-year-old male presents to your office with an intensely pruritic rash 2 days after returning from a camping trip in the woods. On examination you observe a rash on the hands, arms, face, and genitals with vesicles and a few bullae. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate treatment option?
missed_items_detail.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "Topical clobetasol, applied twice daily to affected areas until symptoms resolve"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Oral methylprednisolone, starting at 1 mg/kg and tapering over 5 days"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Oral prednisone, starting at 1 mg/kg and tapering over 15 days"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Subcutaneous triamcinolone, 80 mg once"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "Draining the bullae and large vesicles"}]
missed_items_detail.correct_text: Oral prednisone, starting at 1 mg/kg and tapering over 15 days
missed_items_detail.explanation: Poison ivy is perhaps the most common cause of allergic contact dermatitis in the United States, causing up to 10 million persons annually to seek medical care. Caused by the oleoresin urushiol that is present in Toxicodendron species (poison ivy, sumac, oak), allergic contact dermatitis is a type IV delayed hypersensitivity reaction. Symptoms develop 12”“72 hours after exposure. Prior sensitization is necessary. The rash has varying presentations, including irregular erythematous patches, linear streaks of vesicles, and larger fluid-filled bullae. The blister fluid does not cause the rash to spread. This illusion of spreading is due to the time variability in when the rash develops (hours to days) based on how much resin was in contact with the skin. Systemic corticosteroids are indicated for facial, genital, or significant vesiculobullous involvement. In type IV hypersensitivity reactions, rebound inflammation occurs if treatment with corticosteroids is insufficient in dose or duration. The most effective treatment in this patient would be a 15-day prednisone taper, starting with 1 mg/kg (60 mg at maximum). A single injection of triamcinolone has variable efficacy and often does not take effect as quickly as oral prednisone; however, if used, it should be administered intramuscularly, never subcutaneously, due to the likelihood of lipoatrophy. Topical corticosteroids, even the most potent formulations, do not work on bullous lesions and are contraindicated on the face and genitals. A common prescription for poison ivy dermatitis is a 5-day dose pack of methylprednisolone. This provides temporary improvement but is frequently followed by a rebound in rash, which indicates an insufficient dose and duration of the corticosteroid. There is no indication for draining the bullae or vesicles in this patient.
missed_items_detail.reference: Dinulos JGH. Habif’s Clinical Dermatology: A Color Guide to Diagnosis and Therapy. 7th ed. Elsevier; 2021:483-524.e1.

missed_items_detail.question_text: On examination she is neurovascularly intact. Her vital signs are normal except for an elevated heart rate of 110 beats/min and a blood pressure of 150/92 mm Hg. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient's injury?
missed_items_detail.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "Discharging home with concussion precautions"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Observation in the ED, and acetaminophen"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Observation in the ED, and ibuprofen"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "CT of the head"}]
missed_items_detail.explanation: This patient likely has an upper gastrointestinal (GI) bleed from erosive gastritis or peptic ulcer disease. This is suggested by a history of abdominal pain and nausea and the consistent use of ibuprofen with alcohol. The patient's BUN is elevated, and his hemoglobin level shows anemia. Given that the patient is hemodynamically stable and his hemoglobin level is above the transfusion threshold, he does not need excessive workup prior to treatment, but guidelines recommend initiating a proton pump inhibitor on presentation with no delay regardless of status for endoscopy. Urgent endoscopy is only indicated in patients with hemodynamic instability and signs of upper GI bleeding. There are no randomized, controlled trials or guidelines to support the use of nasogastric lavage. Current guidelines recommend blood transfusion for patients with upper gastrointestinal bleeding when the hemoglobin level is <7 g/dL in most cases or <8 g/dL (80 g/L) in patients with coronary artery disease, recent cardiac surgery, or hematologic malignancies. Arterial embolization is only performed if endoscopy has failed to control bleeding.
missed_items_detail.reference: Shaughnessy AF. Imaging guidelines for children with mild traumatic brain injury. Am Fam Physician. 2024;110(2):202-203. | Lumba-Brown A, Yeates KO, Sarmiento K, et al. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention guideline on the diagnosis and management of mild traumatic brain injury among children. JAMA Pediatr. 2018;172(11):e182853.

missed_items_detail.question_text: A 26-year-old female presents to your clinic because of headache and vision changes. She has also noticed an increase in the size of her hands and feet, changes to her facial features, carpal tunnel symptoms, proximal muscle weakness, fatigue, and menstrual irregularities. MRI of the brain shows a 7-mm pituitary adenoma. Which one of the following diagnostic tests is most likely to be abnormal in this patient?
missed_items_detail.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "A dexamethasone suppression test"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "A serum prolactin level"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "A TSH level"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "A serum insulin-like growth factor 1 level"}]
missed_items_detail.correct_text: A serum insulin-like growth factor 1 level
missed_items_detail.explanation: Pituitary adenomas are classified according to their primary cell origin and the specific hormones they produce. Somatotroph adenomas secrete growth hormone and cause symptoms consistent with acromegaly, including an increase in hand and foot size, enlargement of the mandible, carpal tunnel symptoms, fatigue, proximal muscle weakness, and menstrual irregularities. Somatotroph adenomas are diagnosed by a high serum insulin-like growth factor 1 level. Corticotroph adenomas secrete corticotropin (ACTH) and cause symptoms consistent with Cushing disease such as mood changes, weight gain, proximal muscle weakness, easy bruising, and menstrual irregularity. Corticotroph adenomas can be diagnosed by an abnormal result on a dexamethasone suppression test. Lactotroph adenomas secrete prolactin and cause symptoms consistent with hyperprolactinemia such as galactorrhea, decreased libido, and oligomenorrhea or amenorrhea. Lactotroph adenomas can be diagnosed with an elevated serum prolactin level. Thyrotroph adenomas secrete TSH and cause symptoms consistent with hyperthyroidism such as weight loss, palpitations, heat intolerance, and anxiety. Thyrotroph adenomas can be diagnosed with elevated TSH, free T , and total T levels. 4 4
missed_items_detail.reference: Robbins MS: Diagnosis and management of headache: A review. JAMA 2021;325(18):1874-1885.

missed_items_detail.question_text: A 72-year-old male presents with a 45-minute history of persistent bleeding from his left nostril. He has a medical history of aortic stenosis with a mechanical aortic valve replacement, coronary artery disease, hypertension, and type 2 diabetes. His medications include hydrochlorothiazide, lisinopril (Zestril), metformin, and warfarin. A physical examination reveals a blood pressure of 170/100 mm Hg without orthostatic change and a heart rate of 90 beats/min. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate first step in the treatment of this patient's nosebleed?
missed_items_detail.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "Digital compression of the lower nares for 20 minutes"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Topical vasoconstrictors"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Anterior nasal packing"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Electrical cauterization"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "Reversal of anticoagulation"}]
missed_items_detail.correct_text: Digital compression of the lower nares for 20 minutes
missed_items_detail.explanation: The initial treatment of a hemodynamically stable patient with epistaxis is to pinch the lower third of the nose for 15”“20 minutes. If digital compression is not successful in stopping the bleeding and an anterior bleeding site is visualized on anterior rhinoscopy, then the next step would be to either apply topical vasoconstrictors or use chemical/electrical cautery. If bleeding continues after local measures fail, then anterior nasal packing would be indicated. Acute reversal of anticoagulation should be considered in a patient with ongoing bleeding who is hemodynamically unstable.
missed_items_detail.reference: Wilson SA, Stem LA, Bruehlman RD: Hypothyroidism: Diagnosis and treatment. Am Fam Physician 2021;103(10):605-613. | Wouk N, Helton M: Abnormal uterine bleeding in premenopausal women. Am Fam Physician 2019;99(7):435-443.

missed_items_detail.question_text: An 80-year-old male presents to your office for follow-up of stable angina. He has a medical history of coronary artery disease with placement of a single drug-eluting stent, and stage 4 chronic kidney disease secondary to hypertension. His most recent echocardiogram demonstrated a normal ejection fraction. He does not want additional cardiovascular intervention and prefers you to handle all of his care in the future. The patient’s current medications include the following: Aspirin, 81 mg daily Carvedilol (Coreg), 12.5 mg twice daily Empagliflozin (Jardiance), 10 mg daily Losartan (Cozaar), 50 mg daily On examination he has a blood pressure of 140/85 mm Hg, a heart rate of 52 beats/min, and an oxygen saturation of 98% on room air. A cardiopulmonary examination is within normal limits and an EKG is unchanged from past EKGs. Which one of the following changes to this patient’s medication regimen would be most appropriate to decrease his anginal symptoms?
missed_items_detail.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "Increasing carvedilol"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Increasing empagliflozin"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Adding clopidogrel (Plavix)"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Adding isosorbide mononitrate"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "Adding ivabradine (Corlanor)"}]
missed_items_detail.explanation: β-Blockers, calcium channel blockers, and long-acting nitrates are first-line medications to decrease symptoms in patients with angina. If symptoms persist despite the use of a maximally tolerated dose of a single first-line medication, an additional drug from a different class should be introduced. Adding isosorbide mononitrate is the most appropriate option for this patient; however, it may decrease his blood pressure and will require close attention on follow-up. Ranolazine is another potential recommendation but is often more costly than alternatives. It is recommended in patients who are already taking medications from 2 other classes. Increasing the patient's carvedilol is not recommended due to his relative bradycardia. Neither increasing empagliflozin nor adding clopidogrel helps to decrease anginal symptoms. Ivabradine is contraindicated in patients with chronic coronary disease and normal ejection fraction and is potentially harmful.
missed_items_detail.reference: Virani SS, Newby LK, Arnold SV, et al. 2023 AHA/ACC/ACCP/ASPC/NLA/PCNA guideline for the management of patients with chronic coronary disease: a report of the American Heart Association/American College of Cardiology Joint Committee on clinical practice guidelines. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2023;82(9):833-955.

missed_items_detail.question_text: A 32-year-old female presents with classic symptoms of mild hypothyroidism including cold intolerance, mild fatigue, and constipation. Her TSH level is 6.0 μU/mL (N 0.4”“4.2) and her free T level is within normal limits. A pregnancy test is negative. 4 Which one of the following factors, if present, would be an indication for prescribing levothyroxine (Synthroid)?
missed_items_detail.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "A repeat TSH level in 4\u201d\u201c6 weeks remains at 6.0 \u03bcU/mL"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "A repeat TSH level in 4\u201d\u201c6 weeks increases to 8.0 \u03bcU/mL"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "An elevated free T level 3"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "An elevated thyroid peroxidase antibody level"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "Treatment-resistant constipation"}]
missed_items_detail.correct_text: An elevated thyroid peroxidase antibody level
missed_items_detail.explanation: Hypothyroidism affects approximately 1 in 300 individuals in the United States, with a higher prevalence among women and older adults. Symptoms can range from mild to life- threatening. Because the signs and symptoms of hypothyroidism can be nonspecific, diagnosis is based on blood levels of TSH and free T . Subclinical hypothyroidism is defined 4 by a TSH level that is abnormal but <10 μU/mL while the free T level remains within the 4 normal range. Nonpregnant patients with subclinical hypothyroidism should not be treated with thyroid hormone replacement therapy unless the thyroid peroxidase antibody level is elevated or the TSH level is >10 μU/mL (SOR A). Evaluating the total or free T level is not routinely indicated. Treatment-resistant 3 depression is an indication for treating subclinical hypothyroidism; however, this is not the case for treatment-resistant constipation.
missed_items_detail.reference: Wilson SA, Stem LA, Bruehlman RD. Hypothyroidism: diagnosis and treatment. Am Fam Physician. 2021;103(10):605-613.

missed_items_detail.question_text: A 45-year-old female presents with a 1-year history of central facial erythema, telangiectasias, and inflammatory pustules and papules. The symptoms are worsened by alcohol and excessive sun exposure. You diagnose rosacea. She has not had any previous success with moisturizers, aggressive sun protection, or mild facial soap. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate first step in the management of this condition?
missed_items_detail.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "Topical brimonidine (Mirvaso)"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Topical cyclosporine"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Topical metronidazole"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Oral doxycycline"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "Laser or light-based therapy"}]
missed_items_detail.explanation: Topical metronidazole is a first-line treatment for rosacea. The cost and side-effect profile of topical metronidazole make it most appropriate. Topical azelaic acid is also a first-line treatment for rosacea. Oral doxycycline is a reasonable next step if topical metronidazole is not successful in managing symptoms. The side-effect profile of doxycycline makes it less desirable than topical metronidazole. Additionally, increased sun sensitivity is a common side effect of doxycycline that is problematic for management because rosacea is generally clinically worse in the warmer months. Laser and light-based therapies are second-line treatments for rosacea. They are reasonable options for patients who have not had success with topical metronidazole or azelaic acid. Brimonidine is an α-2 agonist that is another reasonable option in a patient who has not had success with topical metronidazole or topical azelaic acid. However, due to its cost and tendency to exacerbate skin irritation, it is considered a second-line treatment option. Topical cyclosporine is another second-line treatment that has been recalcitrant to first-line options.
missed_items_detail.reference: Frazier W, Zemtsov RK, Ge Y. Rosacea: common questions and answers. Am Fam Physician. 2024;109(6):533-542. | Searle T, Al-Niaimi F, Ali FR. Rosacea. Br J Hosp Med. 2021;82(2):1-8.

missed_items_detail.question_text: A 67-year-old female presents for a wellness examination. She has not been seen for medical care for several years and reports that her last Papanicolaou test was 15 years ago. She does not recall any medical problems and has no known history of abnormal cervical cytology in the past. According to the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force, which one of the following should you recommend regarding further cervical cancer screening?
missed_items_detail.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "No further screening due to the patient's age"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Cervical cytology alone now, with no further screening if the result is normal"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Cervical cytology alone now, then repeated in 3 years with no further screening if the results are normal"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Cervical cytology with HPV co-testing now, with no further screening if the results are normal"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "Cervical cytology with HPV co-testing now, then repeated in 5 years with no further screening if the results are normal"}]
missed_items_detail.correct_text: Cervical cytology with HPV co-testing now, then repeated in 5 years with no further screening if the results are normal
missed_items_detail.explanation: The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force recommends against cervical cancer screening in women older than 65 years only if they have had adequate prior screening. Adequate screening is defined as 3 consecutive negative cytology results or 2 consecutive negative co-testing results within 10 years before stopping screening, with the most recent test occurring within 5 years. Because this patient has not had adequate prior screening, she requires either 2 consecutive negative co-testing results that are 5 years apart or 3 consecutive negative cytology results that are 3 years apart before stopping screening.
missed_items_detail.reference: Final Recommendation Statement: Lung Cancer: Screening. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2021.

missed_items_detail.question_text: A healthy 58-year-old patient presents for an annual health maintenance examination.
